subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Bachelor’s Degree
Completed degree in Business Administration with a focus on Sales and Marketing
Attended university from ages 18 to 22, graduating on time
Participated in a sales internship during undergraduate studies
Attended workshops on technical product sales and CRM software

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Job title: Sales Representative, Wholesale and Manufacturing, Technical and Scientific Products
Role involves prospecting, engaging, and supporting customers purchasing technical products
Responsible for maintaining and updating daily sales transactions
Acts as a point-of-contact for both new and returning customers
Supports sales forecasting and reporting processes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in the technical and scientific products sector
Industry focus: Wholesale and manufacturing of specialized equipment
Sells products to a mix of B2B and B2C customers
Operates primarily in the technology and industrial supply market

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Employed by a mid-sized company with approximately 100-300 employees
Company has a collaborative team environment
Organizational structure includes sales, marketing, logistics, and technical support teams
Work setting is a combination of in-store and remote collaboration

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Business focuses on wholesale and retail sales of technical and scientific products
Market segment includes industrial, educational, and research clients
Company operates regionally with some national clients
Products include laboratory equipment, measurement tools, and technical devices

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has 3-5 years of experience as a sales representative
Started in current role shortly after completing bachelor’s degree
Progressed from sales associate to full sales representative within the company

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Considered an early-career professional
Holds a non-managerial, individual contributor position
Has mentoring responsibilities for newer sales team members
Participates in ongoing sales training and development programs

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Welcomes customers to the store in a warm and friendly manner
Inputs daily sales transactions into Google Sheets and CRM systems
Provides product information, technical guidance, and support to customers
Prepares and follows up on sales quotations and proposals
Collaborates with team members to track sales targets and progress

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends approximately 60% of time on direct customer interactions (in-person, phone, email)
20% of time is dedicated to updating sales records and CRM data
10% of time allocated to internal team meetings and collaboration
Remaining 10% spent on learning about new products and process improvements

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Has autonomy in managing daily interactions and prioritizing customer follow-ups
Makes independent decisions on routine sales processes and minor discounts
Major pricing or contract negotiations require manager approval
Allowed to suggest process improvements and new product offerings

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Greeting and building rapport with customers
Demonstrating product features and benefits
Updating sales data in Google Sheets or Excel
Following established sales scripts or guidelines

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Handling technically detailed product questions from specialized clients
Managing large or custom orders requiring special approvals
Addressing complex customer complaints or escalations
Ensuring data accuracy across multiple sales and CRM systems

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Daily entry of sales transactions into spreadsheets
Generating routine sales performance reports
Updating and maintaining customer contact information
Sending standard follow-up emails after purchases

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Enjoys face-to-face customer interactions and problem-solving
Finds product demonstrations and technical discussions engaging
Considers repetitive data entry and report generation tedious
Least enjoys administrative paperwork unrelated to sales

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Manual sales data entry is time-consuming and prone to errors
Difficulty accessing CRM tools remotely at times
Occasional duplication of customer records between systems
Coordinating with other departments for special orders can be slow

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Feels most productive when engaged in customer-facing activities
Higher skill level in rapport-building leads to increased sales conversions
Productivity drops when bogged down with repetitive administrative tasks
Enjoyment is closely linked to opportunities for learning and meaningful interactions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Open and enthusiastic about adopting new technologies
Sees new tools as opportunities to improve efficiency
Actively seeks out updates and tips for CRM and spreadsheet software
Has a positive outlook on technology-driven change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Considers self quick to adapt to new digital tools
Has successfully transitioned from desktop Excel to Google Sheets
Comfortable exploring new features or updates independently
Occasionally assists colleagues in learning new software

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to dedicate several hours monthly for relevant software training
Prefers hands-on tutorials or interactive modules
Participated in company-offered CRM and Google Workspace trainings
Balances training time with ongoing sales responsibilities

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by the potential for time savings and improved sales results
Learning is prioritized when new tools directly impact daily workflow
High workload periods can delay adoption of new technologies
Sometimes hesitant if benefits of a tool are unclear or poorly communicated

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peers' positive experiences encourage trying new tools
Management support and endorsement play a key role in adoption
Team discussions and informal sharing of tech tips are common
Feedback from early adopters within the team influences personal usage

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses Google Sheets daily for recording and sharing sales data
Relies on CRM platforms such as Salesforce or a proprietary CRM
Regularly uses email clients (Gmail/Outlook) for customer communication
Occasionally uses Excel for advanced data analysis

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Smartphone used for remote spreadsheet access and customer calls
POS (Point of Sale) system for transaction processing
Company intranet for accessing product documentation
Cloud storage (e.g., Google Drive) for sharing sales materials

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Familiar with Salesforce and Zoho CRM systems
Comfortable navigating Google Workspace tools (Docs, Sheets, Drive)
Experienced in working with POS systems typical for retail/wholesale
Understands basic integrations between CRM and spreadsheet tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Occasional syncing issues between CRM and Google Sheets
Manual export/import needed for some sales reports
Duplicate data entry required when systems lack integration
Team occasionally faces challenges integrating new tools with existing platforms

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Understands basic concepts such as natural language processing and machine learning
Knows what large language models (LLMs) are and common use cases
Familiar with AI-generated content and basic automation principles
Can explain the difference between AI automation and traditional macros

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Uses ChatGPT for generating sales email drafts and product FAQs
Occasionally uses Google Bard for quick research and information summarization
Has tried Grammarly's AI-powered writing assistant for professional communication
Tested AI-based lead scoring features within CRM platform

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Uses LLMs occasionally, about 2-4 times per week for specific tasks
Applies AI tools for composing responses to customer inquiries
Employs AI to brainstorm product descriptions and social media posts
Uses AI to automate repetitive data analysis tasks during monthly reporting

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: Successfully used ChatGPT to quickly draft a technical product FAQ, saving 2 hours
AI-generated email templates occasionally required heavy editing for tone and accuracy
Once received inaccurate product details from an AI tool, prompting more manual verification
Positive experience with AI-powered lead scoring, which improved targeting for follow-ups

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: No formal company-wide AI training yet, but informal peer support is available
Shares AI tool tips and best practices during team meetings
Management encourages experimentation with safe AI tools
Occasionally consults online resources or webinars for AI learning

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers to explore new AI tools independently at first
Appreciates step-by-step guidance for more complex AI workflows
Enjoys a mix of self-directed use and collaborative problem-solving with team members
Leverages help documentation and online communities when stuck

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI primarily as an assistant to streamline repetitive tasks
Occasionally treats AI as an advisor for brainstorming sales strategies
Prefers to maintain final decision-making authority over AI recommendations
Uses AI to augment, not replace, core interpersonal skills in sales

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Open to experimenting with new AI-driven workflows that show clear benefits
Willing to pilot new AI features introduced by CRM and productivity tools
Assesses the impact of AI changes before full-scale adoption
Advocates for gradual integration rather than sudden workflow overhaul

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Prefers conversational AI interfaces for brainstorming and writing tasks
Uses command-based interfaces for data-related automations
Finds chat-style interfaces more intuitive for daily sales support
Switches between modes depending on task complexity

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Many product recommendations require nuanced understanding of customer needs
Technical product sales often involve interpreting specifications beyond what AI can provide
Trusts AI with generic tasks, but not highly specialized advice
Tacit knowledge of local client relationships is crucial in closing deals

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI-generated drafts and suggestions for routine communication
Double-checks AI outputs for technical accuracy and appropriateness
Does not rely solely on AI for pricing or contract recommendations
Views AI as a supportive tool, not a decision-maker, for critical sales processes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI to automate repetitive data entry and initial drafts
Human oversight is valued for customer interactions and negotiations
Seeks a 70/30 split: AI for routine, human for complex, relationship-driven tasks
Wants AI to surface insights, but humans to make final sales decisions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: Accepts high automation for administrative and reporting tasks
Finds automation threatening when it risks reducing human contact with clients
Prefers manual review for high-value or custom orders
Sees automation as beneficial when it frees time for relationship building

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes AI enhances efficiency but does not replace interpersonal sales skills
Views technical product knowledge as more important despite automation
Sees data analysis skills becoming more valuable with AI-driven insights
Thinks AI may reduce the need for manual reporting, but not for client engagement

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Developing skills in prompt engineering to get better AI outputs
Learning to critically evaluate AI-generated content for accuracy
Responsible for integrating AI suggestions into customer communications
Gaining familiarity with AI-driven data visualization tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Low concern about AI replacing the entire sales role in the short term
Moderately concerned about automation of repetitive administrative tasks
Believes high-value relationship management cannot be automated
Watches industry trends for signs of increased automation in sales

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: Always reviews AI-generated emails before sending to customers
Manual approval required for AI-generated sales reports
CRM system allows editing of AI-suggested lead scores
Uses AI as a first draft, but maintains human control over final outputs

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Company encourages careful experimentation with AI tools
Team now openly discusses pros and cons of new AI features
Management is developing guidelines for responsible AI use in sales
Policies emphasize transparency and human oversight for AI-driven workflows

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Believes AI will lead to greater efficiency and productivity across industries
Expects AI to create new types of jobs in sales and customer support
Thinks AI may widen the gap between tech-savvy and traditional workers
Sees potential for AI to improve the customer experience in retail and B2B sales

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Believes ethical use of AI requires transparency about its role in communications
Concerned about data privacy when using AI for customer information
Thinks AI should not be used to deceive or mislead customers
Supports clear boundaries for what AI can and cannot do in sales

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Would benefit from an AI-powered tool that automatically syncs CRM and spreadsheet data
Desires smarter AI-driven customer segmentation for targeted promotions
Wants an AI tool that can summarize and suggest action items from sales meetings
Seeks improved language support for multilingual sales communications

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Expects more routine sales tasks to be automated by AI-driven platforms
Anticipates a greater focus on consultative and relationship-based selling
Sees a need for advanced digital literacy and AI collaboration skills
Believes data-driven decision-making will become central to the sales role

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Implement regular training sessions on new AI tools and features
Establish clear policies for ethical and responsible AI use
Encourage employee feedback and experimentation with AI-driven processes
Invest in integrated platforms to reduce manual data entry and duplication
